content.text: What it is

BDD automation aligned with eval suites is the practice of structuring Gherkin-based acceptance tests and LLM evaluation datasets so they reinforce each other rather than measuring the same things twice. BDD scenarios — written in the Given-When-Then format — express agreed acceptance criteria for named features. Eval suites — collections of input/expected-output pairs with scoring functions — measure quality across a broader distribution of inputs. Aligning the two means each provides a signal the other cannot: BDD provides feature-level pass/fail with a business-readable specification; evals provide distributional quality metrics across hundreds of examples.

The alignment is achieved at the data layer, the execution layer, and the reporting layer. At the data layer, the BDD examples table in a Scenario Outline is sourced from the eval dataset, creating a direct link between acceptance criteria and evaluation examples. At the execution layer, BDD step definitions are written to call the same scoring functions used in the eval suite, so the pass/fail criterion for a BDD Then step is the same numerical threshold used in batch evaluation. At the reporting layer, both signal types are aggregated into a single quality dashboard so that stakeholders see one coherent picture of feature quality.
Why it matters

When BDD automation and eval suites are maintained independently, teams create two inconsistent quality signals. The BDD suite may pass because a handful of manually curated examples happen to work with the current model. The eval suite may flag a quality regression on the same feature because it covers a broader input distribution. If the reporting systems do not connect these signals, engineers investigating the eval regression must cross-reference the BDD spec manually to understand whether the regression violates the accepted feature criteria.

Alignment also prevents threshold drift: when BDD Then steps use hardcoded numeric thresholds defined independently of the eval suite's scoring criteria, the two systems may apply different standards for what counts as a passing response. Over time, this divergence makes it impossible to answer the question 'does this feature meet its acceptance criteria?' because the answer depends on which measurement system you look at.
Benefits
Shared scoring functions eliminate threshold drift between BDD acceptance criteria and eval quality metrics
Sourcing BDD examples from the eval dataset ensures acceptance tests cover a representative distribution, not just cherry-picked cases
A single quality dashboard reduces context switching for engineers triaging quality regressions
Alignment makes it clear which eval failures are acceptance criteria violations (requiring a fix) versus quality improvements below the threshold (requiring a threshold negotiation)
Reduces maintenance: when the eval dataset grows, BDD Scenario Outlines automatically cover the new examples without manual updates to feature files
Trade-offs and limitations
BDD feature files generated from eval datasets can become very large; the Gherkin Examples table may contain hundreds of rows, which is unwieldy for human review
Scoring functions from the eval suite (LLM-as-judge, embedding similarity) are non-deterministic; BDD PR CI must use VCR cassettes to avoid flaky tests
The eval dataset must be of high quality before it can serve as the data source for BDD acceptance tests; misaligned examples will produce misleading BDD results
Tight coupling between BDD and eval infrastructure increases the complexity of each system; a change to the eval scoring function requires a corresponding change to the BDD step definitions
How it works

The alignment is implemented by building three bridges between the BDD framework and the eval infrastructure. The first bridge is the data bridge: a script that reads the eval dataset and generates the Examples table rows for each Gherkin Scenario Outline. The script filters the eval dataset to include only examples that are relevant to the feature covered by the Scenario Outline, selecting a representative sample across difficulty levels (easy, medium, hard, edge case).

The second bridge is the scoring bridge: BDD Then step definitions import and call the same scorer module used by the eval suite. When the BDD step says 'the response quality score exceeds 0.80', it calls score_response(actual, reference, criteria) — the same function used in the nightly batch eval — rather than a bespoke string comparison. This ensures the BDD passing criterion is identical to the eval passing criterion.

The third bridge is the reporting bridge: both BDD test results and eval run results are published to the same metrics store, with feature name as the common key. The quality dashboard reads both sources and renders a combined view showing BDD acceptance status alongside the eval quality trend line for the same feature.
Architecture

attrs.alt: BDD and eval suite alignment architecture: shared eval dataset and scorer module feed both the BDD suite and the batch eval suite, with results flowing to a unified quality dashboard

content.text: The shared eval dataset and scorer module sit at the centre of the architecture. Both the BDD suite and the batch eval suite draw from these shared resources, ensuring they apply the same quality criteria. Results from both systems flow to the quality dashboard, which presents feature-level acceptance status alongside the quality trend line from the eval suite.
When to use it

Apply BDD-eval alignment when a feature has both a product acceptance specification (expressible as Gherkin) and a quality metric tracked over time (expressible as an eval dataset). Not every AI feature needs both: a simple classification feature with a single acceptance criterion can be covered by BDD alone. Features that warrant alignment are those with continuous quality monitoring requirements — hallucination rate, answer relevance, tool call accuracy — that need to be tracked as the model evolves across releases.
How to implement
Define the feature's acceptance criteria as Gherkin Scenario Outlines with an Examples table placeholder
Create or identify the corresponding eval dataset for the feature, including examples tagged by difficulty tier
Write a dataset-to-examples script that reads the eval dataset and generates the Gherkin Examples table rows, selecting a balanced sample per difficulty tier
Extract the eval suite's scoring function into a shared module; import it in the BDD step definition's Then step
Add a VCR cassette recording for the BDD @stub scenarios so the PR CI path is deterministic and fast
Configure both the BDD suite and the eval suite to publish results to the same metrics store keyed by feature name
Build a quality dashboard view that shows BDD acceptance status (pass/fail per scenario) alongside eval quality trends (score percentile over time)
Examples

# scripts/generate_bdd_examples.py
# Reads the eval dataset and writes the Gherkin Examples table

import json
import random

EVAL_DATASET = 'evals/expense_categorisation/dataset.json'
OUTPUT_FEATURE = 'features/expense_categorisation.feature'
SAMPLE_PER_TIER = 5  # pick 5 examples per difficulty tier

with open(EVAL_DATASET) as f:
    examples = json.load(f)['examples']

# Group by difficulty tier
tiers = {}
for ex in examples:
    tier = ex.get('tier', 'medium')
    tiers.setdefault(tier, []).append(ex)

# Sample from each tier
sampled = []
for tier_name, tier_examples in tiers.items():
    sampled.extend(random.sample(tier_examples, min(SAMPLE_PER_TIER, len(tier_examples))))

# Write to Gherkin Examples table
lines = [
    '      | amount | description | expected_category | min_confidence |'
]
for ex in sampled:
    lines.append(
        f"      | {ex['amount']} | {ex['description']!r} | {ex['expected_category']!r} | {ex['min_confidence']} |"
    )

print('\n'.join(lines))
# In CI: write these lines into the feature file before running pytest-bdd

# evals/shared/scorer.py — used by both BDD step definitions and eval suite

def score_response(actual: dict, reference: dict, criteria: list[str]) -> float:
    """
    Returns a quality score in [0, 1].
    Criteria is a list of dimensions: 'category_accuracy', 'confidence', 'explanation_quality'
    """
    scores = []
    if 'category_accuracy' in criteria:
        scores.append(1.0 if actual.get('category') == reference.get('expected_category') else 0.0)
    if 'confidence' in criteria:
        min_conf = reference.get('min_confidence', 0.80)
        scores.append(1.0 if actual.get('confidence', 0) >= min_conf else 0.0)
    if 'explanation_quality' in criteria:
        # LLM-as-judge: skipped in VCR stub mode; returns cassette value
        scores.append(actual.get('_judge_score', 0.8))
    return sum(scores) / len(scores) if scores else 0.0


# step_definitions/expense_steps.py (BDD usage)
from evals.shared.scorer import score_response

@then(parsers.parse('the overall quality score exceeds {threshold:f}'))
def assert_quality(context, threshold):
    score = score_response(
        context['result'],
        context['reference'],
        criteria=['category_accuracy', 'confidence']
    )
    assert score > threshold, f'Quality score {score:.2f} below threshold {threshold}'
Real-world example

A financial services firm had a contract review AI with 120 eval examples and a Gherkin feature file with 6 manually written scenarios. The two systems were maintained separately. When the underlying model was updated, the eval suite showed a 4-point drop in the 'key clause extraction' metric, but the BDD suite passed because its 6 hand-picked examples happened to be the easy cases that the new model still handled correctly. By aligning the two — generating the BDD Examples table from the eval dataset and using the same scorer — the BDD suite immediately caught the regression, failing on 8 of the 30 sampled eval examples. The team could then identify the specific clause types that regressed rather than spending hours cross-referencing two separate reports.
Common mistakes
Running all eval examples as BDD scenarios: include only a representative sample; hundreds of scenarios in a Gherkin file defeats the readability purpose
Hardcoding thresholds in BDD steps that differ from the eval suite's acceptance criteria: use the same constant from a shared configuration module
Using live LLM calls in the BDD PR CI path: the scorer module must be called via VCR cassette stub to produce deterministic results
Generating BDD Examples tables but not regenerating them when the eval dataset is updated: add the generation script to the CI pipeline so the tables stay current
Best practices
Keep the shared scorer module as a standalone Python package with its own tests; both BDD and eval depend on it via a versioned import
Sample BDD examples from each difficulty tier of the eval dataset so the acceptance test covers easy, medium, hard, and edge case inputs
Record VCR cassettes using real model output, not synthetic data; the cassette should reflect what the model actually produces for each sampled example
Publish BDD pass/fail results alongside eval score trends in the same dashboard view; feature name is the key that links the two signals
Regenerate the BDD Examples table whenever the eval dataset grows significantly — adding 20% more examples warrants a resample
Performance and scalability

The BDD suite using VCR cassettes will run in under 60 seconds even with 30 sampled examples, because cassette replay is an in-memory operation. The batch eval suite runs the full dataset against a real model; at 120 examples with a 2-second mean latency, this is approximately 4 minutes with parallelism. Separate the two execution contexts: BDD on every PR (fast), eval suite nightly (thorough). Increase the BDD sample size (examples per tier) only when the PR CI budget allows it without breaking the 5-minute gate target.
Tooling and ecosystem
Tool
Role in alignment
Notes
pytest-bdd
BDD test runner
Executes Gherkin scenarios; imports scorer module in Then steps
VCR.py
Cassette replay for PR CI
Deterministic fast execution without live LLM calls
promptfoo / Braintrust
Eval suite runner
Runs batch eval on full dataset; scorer module can be a custom evaluator
Grafana / Datadog
Quality dashboard
Aggregates BDD pass/fail and eval score trends in a single view
Comparison with alternatives
Approach
Strengths
Weaknesses
BDD-eval alignment (this topic)
Consistent thresholds; shared data source; unified reporting
More setup; scorer module must be maintained as shared package
Independent BDD + eval
Simpler; each system independent
Threshold drift; two separate dashboards; confusing regression triage
Eval only, no BDD
Scales well; covers large input distributions
No business-readable acceptance specification; non-technical stakeholders cannot verify
Security and reliability

The eval dataset used as the source for BDD Examples tables may contain sensitive test inputs that reveal the system's behaviour under adversarial or edge case conditions. Apply the same repository access controls to the eval dataset as to production data; do not store real user inputs in eval datasets. Annotate eval examples with synthetic identifiers rather than real customer names, account numbers, or transaction IDs. When the BDD feature file is published as living documentation, ensure that the Examples table rows containing sensitive domain content are either excluded or redacted in the published report.
Dataset sampling strategy for BDD coverage

The number of eval examples to include in the BDD Examples table involves a trade-off between coverage and readability. A sample of 5 examples per difficulty tier (easy, medium, hard, edge case) gives 20 total rows — enough to detect regressions across the difficulty distribution while keeping the Gherkin file reviewable. A larger sample increases fault detection sensitivity but reduces the readability that makes Gherkin valuable as a shared specification format.
Tier 1 (easy): prototypical examples with unambiguous expected output and high confidence; 3–5 examples per scenario
Tier 2 (medium): moderate ambiguity; the model correctly handles these with the current prompt; 3–5 examples
Tier 3 (hard): inputs near the capability boundary; the model passes these with the current version but previous versions did not; 2–3 examples
Tier 4 (edge case): inputs that test boundary conditions and refusal behaviour; 2–3 examples
Resample when the eval dataset grows by more than 20% or when a new difficulty tier is added to the dataset taxonomy
Handling scorer non-determinism in BDD CI

LLM-as-judge scorers used in the eval suite are inherently non-deterministic: the same response may receive slightly different scores across runs due to the judge model's temperature. Running these live in BDD PR CI produces flaky tests — tests that intermittently fail for non-code reasons. The solution is twofold: record VCR cassettes for the LLM-as-judge calls alongside the agent calls, and set the judge model's temperature to zero for cassette recording to produce maximally reproducible scores.

For the nightly live eval run, accept a small variance window: if the score is within ±0.02 of the threshold, flag it as a warning rather than a hard failure. This variance window prevents a single-point failure from blocking release when the regression is within the natural measurement uncertainty of the scorer. Hard failures are reserved for score drops that exceed the variance window — typically indicative of a genuine capability regression rather than scorer noise.
Record VCR cassettes for both agent calls and LLM-as-judge calls when running BDD PR CI
Set judge model temperature to 0 during cassette recording to produce deterministic score values
Define a variance window (±0.02) for the live eval run; scores within the window generate a warning, not a hard failure
Run the live eval multiple times and average the scores when a result falls near the threshold to reduce noise-driven false positives
Triage workflow for BDD-eval divergence

When the BDD suite passes but the eval suite flags a regression on the same feature, the divergence must be triaged to determine the cause. The most common cause is sampling gap: the BDD sample did not include examples from the difficulty tier where the regression occurred. The triage process examines which eval examples failed, identifies their difficulty tier, and checks whether any BDD Examples table rows correspond to that tier. If the tier is absent from the BDD table, the resolution is to add examples from that tier to the table and regenerate the cassettes.
Compare the failing eval examples' difficulty tier against the BDD Examples table; absent tiers indicate a sampling gap
If the BDD table includes examples from the failing tier but BDD still passed, the cassette may be stale — re-record using the current model output
If BDD Examples were drawn from the failing tier and BDD still passed with live model calls, the eval dataset may include adversarial or mislabelled examples — review the ground truth
Document the triage outcome in the quality dashboard run notes so the next engineer facing the same pattern can resolve it faster
Integrating alignment into the CI pipeline

The data bridge between the eval dataset and the BDD feature file must be maintained automatically in CI to prevent drift. A pre-test CI step runs the generate_bdd_examples script, overwrites the Examples table in the feature file, and fails the build if the generated content differs from the committed version. This check ensures that engineers who add examples to the eval dataset also update the committed BDD feature file, keeping the two artefacts in sync at every commit.
Add generate_bdd_examples to the CI pre-test stage; diff the output against the committed feature file; fail if they diverge
Regenerate VCR cassettes automatically when the Examples table changes; never allow stale cassettes to persist in the repository
Publish the final BDD pass/fail summary to the metrics store as a post-test CI step, keyed by feature name and commit SHA for traceability

The divergence triage workflow should be triggered automatically when the BDD suite result and the eval suite result differ by more than a configurable tolerance for the same feature. Configure the quality dashboard to generate a triage ticket in the project tracker when this condition is detected, prefilled with the feature name, the BDD result, the eval result, the diff in failing example tiers, and a link to the relevant CI runs. This removes the burden of manual cross-referencing from the on-call engineer and ensures that every BDD-eval divergence is formally investigated and resolved rather than silently accepted.

Teams that establish a BDD-eval alignment practice report fewer surprise regressions at UAT time, because the alignment ensures that the BDD acceptance gate reflects the same quality standard that the eval suite enforces. Features that consistently pass BDD acceptance with aligned criteria are substantially more likely to pass UAT without significant defects, because the sampled BDD examples cover the difficulty distribution that real user inputs fall within — not just the easy cases that manual scenario authors tend to write.
Key takeaways
Align BDD and eval suites by sharing two resources: the eval dataset (as BDD Examples source) and the scorer module (as the Then step assertion function)
Threshold drift between BDD and eval is eliminated when both systems use the same scoring function with the same thresholds
Sample BDD examples from the eval dataset by difficulty tier so acceptance tests cover a representative distribution, not just easy cases
Use VCR cassettes for BDD PR CI so the shared scorer module runs deterministically without live LLM calls
Publish BDD acceptance status and eval score trends to a unified dashboard — feature name is the key that links the two signals
